translations.en: Al-Husayn (peace be upon him) looked right and left and saw no one remaining. He raised his head to the sky and said: 'O Allah! You see what is being done to the descendants of Your Prophet.' The Banu Kilab came between him and the water. An arrow was shot and struck his throat. He fell from his horse. He took the arrow and cast it away. He tried to catch the blood with his hand until his palm was full, then smeared it on his head and beard, saying: 'I will meet Allah, Almighty and Majestic, oppressed and smeared in my own blood.' Then he fell upon his left cheek. The enemies of Allah — Sinan b. Anas al-Iyadi and Shimr b. Dhil Jawshan (may Allah curse them both) — advanced with men from the Levant. Some said to one another: 'What are you waiting for? Put the man to rest.' Sinan b. Anas came down, took al-Husayn's beard, and began striking his throat with the sword, saying: 'By Allah! I cut off your head knowing you are the son of the Messenger of Allah and that you have the best father and mother.' The horse of al-Husayn came forward with its mane covered in his blood, running and neighing. The daughters of the Prophet heard its neighs and saw the horse without a rider. They knew al-Husayn had been killed. Umm Kulthum bt. al-Husayn came out, wailing: 'O Muhammada! This is al-Husayn in the open, stripped of his turban and cloak.' Sinan came forward until he brought the head of al-Husayn b. 'Ali to 'Ubaydullah b. Ziyad, saying [verse]: 'Fill my saddlebag with silver and gold, for I have killed the veiled king; I have killed the one with the best mother and father, and the best lineage.' So 'Ubaydullah said to him: 'Woe to you! If you knew he has the best father and mother, why did you kill him?!' He ordered his neck to be struck, and Allah hastened his soul to the Fire. Then Ibn Ziyad sent for Umm Kulthum bt. al-Husayn and said: 'Praise be to Allah who killed your men — so how do you see what has happened to you?' She said: 'O Ibn Ziyad! If your eye is gladdened by the killing of al-Husayn, then the eye of his grandfather has long been gladdened by him — he used to kiss him and carry him on his shoulders. O Ibn Ziyad! Prepare an answer for his grandfather, for he will be your adversary tomorrow.'
